what_it_does: Documents a design risk called "anchor rot": durable annotation envelopes that capture content, context, and an address will mislead future agents when the underlying document or UI keeps changing, because detecting staleness is not the same as re-anchoring to the correct new location.
observations: The envelope captures content, surrounding context, and a path/address at annotation time.
Documents and UI are intentionally mutable because agents act on feedback and edit the source.
A stored path can point to moved or deleted content after an edit, causing silent mis-edits later.
The brief's content-signature freshness (design map A7) detects that something changed but does not re-locate the annotation.
Re-anchoring against a shifting tree is compared to the unsolved problem of Google Docs/PDF annotations breaking on re-layout.
ui:// element identities are said not to survive component refactor (#3).
Append-only mark-is-truth means rotted anchors cannot be cleaned up, only layered with more observations.
standouts: Detecting staleness is not the same as re-anchoring.
Anchor rot is presented as an inherent property of durable observations on mutating targets, not a later bug.
Append-only truth makes rotted anchors permanent and irremediable.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item YAML frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, and timestamps.
Uses Markdown heading and bullet argumentation.
References external design artifacts by shorthand (design map A7, #3, the brief) and a parent item via links.
Uses a concrete persona example ("Tim comments...") to illustrate silent failure.
concerns: Relies on undefined local terms such as envelope, brief, design map A7, and #3.
States that "Tim's own standing rules forbid" silent failures without citing those rules in the file.
Mixes problem description with critique of design decisions without proposing a concrete mitigation.
Assumes the reader already knows the system's anchoring and freshness mechanisms.
notes: A crisp warning that durable annotations on mutable sources will rot, and that freshness checks alone cannot relocate them.
questions: What is the exact data model of an envelope (fields, anchor types, content signature)?
Which brief and design map A7 are referenced, and where are they defined?
What does the ui:// element identity scheme look like, and what is issue #3?
What are Tim's standing rules and where are they recorded?
Is there any proposed mitigation or acceptance criteria for anchor rot?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches content: the title claims anchor rot will mislead agents by design over time, and the body explains why with examples.
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to elevate anchor rot from a tractable bug to a systemic, unresolved design risk in an agent-edited channel-memory system, and to argue that the brief's freshness mechanism does not solve it.
novelty: Frames anchor rot as an intentional, irremediable property of append-only annotations rather than a bug that can be fixed later.
contribution: A clear articulation of the difference between detecting annotation staleness and re-locating an annotation on a mutating document or UI tree, plus the warning that append-only records make rotted anchors permanent.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item arguing that durable annotation envelopes anchored to mutable documents and UI components will silently mislead agents over time because staleness detection does not solve re-anchoring.
intention_for_embedding: To flag anchor rot as an inherent, unresolved risk in an append-only, agent-edited channel-memory system.
